title: Were On The Road To Dohwhere

jokes.text: School hasn't even started yet. What can I say? I missed you, Myra.
jokes.setup: Bart is already in trouble before school starts
jokes.punchline: His flirtatious excuse to the secretary
jokes.explanation: Classic Bart charm - using smooth talk to deflect from trouble. Good character integration.

jokes.text: How's things with you and Frank? That bum! He says his divorce will be final next week. So why is she pregnant again?
jokes.setup: Bart asks casually about Myra's relationship
jokes.punchline: Uncomfortable overshare about adult relationship drama
jokes.explanation: Excellent absurdist moment - 10-year-old having adult conversation with secretary. The casual tone makes it funnier.

jokes.text: Ah, 1995. A fine year for Camrys. No one would ever guess you spent three months in a lake.
jokes.punchline: Casual mention of the car's traumatic past
jokes.explanation: Perfect Skinner - finding the positive in disaster with bureaucratic precision. The specificity of 'three months' sells it.

jokes.text: Ay, caramba!
jokes.explanation: Classic catchphrase moment - low originality but high character integration and quotability.

jokes.text: Thank you, satan.
jokes.explanation: Perfect economy and character work - Bart's rebellious nature in two words. Subversive without being gratuitous.

jokes.text: I don't mind! Trouble's a form of attention.
jokes.setup: Bart warns Milhouse they could get in trouble
jokes.punchline: Milhouse's pathetic eagerness for any attention
jokes.explanation: Brilliant Milhouse characterization - his desperation turned into pseudo-wisdom. Very quotable insight.

jokes.text: You're usually so stingy and rat-like.
jokes.explanation: Homer's blunt honesty about Moe's character flaws delivered matter-of-factly

jokes.text: Well, I sued the rope company and I got a huge settlement... and a new rope!
jokes.setup: Moe explains his windfall from a failed suicide attempt
jokes.punchline: The rope company gave him a replacement rope
jokes.explanation: Classic dark Moe humor - the absurdity of getting a 'replacement' suicide rope is perfect character comedy.

jokes.text: You could hang a cow with that thing!
jokes.punchline: Measuring rope quality by livestock hanging capacity
jokes.explanation: Good escalation of the rope joke with Barney's oblivious enthusiasm for suicide equipment quality.

jokes.text: It's like we're in the school's lymphatic system and we're the lymph.
jokes.explanation: Perfect Milhouse - turning adventure into science lesson. Shows his character while being genuinely clever.

jokes.text: Look we're having pizza tomorrow! I'm gonna eat a lighter breakfast.
jokes.explanation: Good character comedy - Milhouse's practical approach to exciting discovery. Relatable kid logic.

jokes.text: Yeah, it'd be like going to Amsterdam and not taking a walking tour of famous doors.
jokes.setup: Bart argues they must turn a valve
jokes.punchline: Innocent tourism reference that hints at Amsterdam's reputation
jokes.explanation: Clever adult humor disguised as innocent kid comment. The 'famous doors' euphemism is brilliant writing.

jokes.text: What the F-minus?
jokes.setup: Teacher reacts to steam in classroom
jokes.explanation: Clever wordplay that shows teacher staying in character while expressing frustration. Good economy.

jokes.text: Look, a new kid! Get him!
jokes.setup: Students see someone new during chaos
jokes.explanation: Standard school violence humor. Functional but not particularly clever or original.

jokes.text: Our instruments are rusting! We're going a cappella, people!
jokes.setup: Steam is affecting the band room
jokes.explanation: Good escalation of steam disaster. Teacher maintaining professionalism amid chaos is character-appropriate.

jokes.text: I've got two kinds of wet in my pants!
jokes.explanation: Classic Willie overshare. Good character integration but relies on crude humor for impact.

jokes.text: No water fountain outwits Willie!
jokes.explanation: Perfect Willie - turning simple task into personal battle. Great character voice and quotable.

jokes.text: This wood is more buckled than a pilgrim's hat.
jokes.explanation: Excellent Willie line - the specific historical reference feels authentic to his character. Very quotable.

jokes.text: Sounds like my parents' custody hearing.
jokes.setup: Skinner says he doesn't want Milhouse
jokes.punchline: Milhouse's casual reference to parental rejection
jokes.explanation: Brilliant dark humor from Milhouse. Perfect economy and devastating character insight delivered casually.

jokes.text: This used to be a croissandwich. Now, it's a ham and cheese car crash.
jokes.explanation: Good visual metaphor with nice alliteration. Shows Myra's dramatic personality through food description.

jokes.text: You're in Scottsdale? I'm in Scottsdale! At the Hyatt room 381! Get outta here! I'm in 502!
jokes.punchline: Officials realize they're in the same hotel
jokes.explanation: Good escalating absurdity - bureaucratic meeting becomes neighborly chat

jokes.text: You're in Scottsdale? I'm in Scottsdale! At the Hyatt room 381! Get outta here! I'm in 502! Hey, do you guys have any ice buckets down there? No, we've been using shower caps.
jokes.setup: Two officials on video call discover they're in same hotel
jokes.punchline: Dissolving into casual hotel chat during official business
jokes.explanation: Great escalation from official business to mundane hotel problems. The shower cap detail is perfect.

jokes.text: They use a proven concept 'tough hate' to turn around foul-mouthed hooligans. Then they send them home, quietly ticking away.
jokes.explanation: Good wordplay on 'tough love' with dark implications. The 'ticking' metaphor adds sinister undertone.

jokes.text: Climbing some kind of rope thing. Wearing a backpack. High-fiving the black kid.
jokes.punchline: Clinical description of diverse stock photo elements
jokes.explanation: Brilliant meta-commentary on forced diversity in marketing materials. Skinner's clinical tone sells it.

jokes.text: No wires at all. Except this one.
jokes.punchline: Immediately contradicting himself about the wire
jokes.explanation: Classic Homer obliviousness. Good structure and economy, though the concept is familiar.

jokes.text: What's your system? I don't tell your mother how much I've lost.
jokes.setup: Bart asks about Homer's gambling system
jokes.punchline: Homer's 'system' is just hiding losses from Marge
jokes.explanation: Perfect Homer logic - mistaking deception for strategy. Great setup/punchline structure.

jokes.text: Nobody tells Bart Simpson what to do.
jokes.setup: Flight attendant tells passengers to stay seated
jokes.explanation: Pure Bart character moment. Not particularly original but perfectly in character.

jokes.text: Thanks a lot, 33C. Now we all have to go back to Minneapolis, and I'm very tired.
jokes.explanation: Good deadpan delivery. The personal exhaustion makes it relatable and funny.

jokes.text: Dang. Homer ain't coming. And Nelson saw something funny.
jokes.punchline: Barney's understated disappointment with random Nelson detail
jokes.explanation: Good Barney moment - his simple disappointment and random observation feel authentic.

jokes.text: Stupid Bart makes me drive to Oregon, home of unspoiled forests, birthplace of Matt Groening.
jokes.setup: Homer complaining about driving to Oregon
jokes.explanation: Nice meta-joke. Homer unknowingly referencing his creator while complaining is cleverly done.

jokes.text: We'll get a monkey drunk and push him down the stairs.
jokes.setup: Fun Homer trying to convince Serious Homer to let him out
jokes.explanation: Great absurdist humor. The specificity and casual animal cruelty is peak Homer id.

jokes.text: I'll kill you the way I killed intellectual Homer!
jokes.punchline: Revealing he's already murdered his intellectual side
jokes.explanation: Brilliant dark character insight - explains Homer's stupidity as active suppression. Great psychological comedy.

jokes.text: I'll kill you the way I killed intellectual Homer!
jokes.explanation: Dark humor about Homer's lost intelligence - implies he once was smart

jokes.text: And every one was Stuart Little.
jokes.setup: Marge mentions Homer saved all his book reports
jokes.punchline: Revealing his lack of literary range
jokes.explanation: Good Homer characterization - shows his laziness and lack of intellectual curiosity efficiently.

jokes.text: I'm 38 years old, driving a crappy car, with a son who doesn't respect me, and I'm one Snickers pie away from losing my foot to diabetes. Mmm... Snickers pie!
jokes.setup: Homer's moment of self-awareness about his failures
jokes.punchline: Immediately distracted by the diabetes threat itself
jokes.explanation: Perfect Homer moment - rare self-awareness immediately undermined by his own weakness. Excellent structure.

jokes.text: See, Bart? I told you things could be worse. I'm getting a sweatshirt with Shakespeare's face on it.
jokes.explanation: Decent cringe humor about cultural pretension mixed with tourist kitsch.

jokes.text: Look at me! I use a napkin.
jokes.explanation: Good cringe comedy - kid's pretentious announcement of basic civility is perfectly awful.

jokes.text: They were eating melon for breakfast.
jokes.punchline: Melon for breakfast seems foreign and suspicious to him
jokes.explanation: Good class contrast - healthy eating as exotic behavior to working-class characters

jokes.text: You know, it's legal there for a guy to marry his son.
jokes.punchline: Absurd legal claim about gay marriage
jokes.explanation: Typical Homer ignorance about social issues, but feels dated and relies on cheap shock value.

jokes.text: But could you add a bacon nose? Plus bacon hair, bacon mustache, five o'clock shadow made of bacon bits, and A... bacon body.
jokes.punchline: Escalating bacon requests creating full bacon person
jokes.explanation: Great escalation showing Homer's food obsession. The detailed progression is well-structured.

jokes.text: How 'bout if I just shoved a pig down your throat?
jokes.explanation: Good escalation response with nice economy. Waitress's deadpan frustration works well.

jokes.text: But the bacon man lives in a bacon house! No, he doesn't!
jokes.punchline: Waitress firmly rejecting his bacon world-building
jokes.explanation: Good continuation of bacon escalation. Homer's childlike persistence vs adult reality.

jokes.text: What?! No one dashie-dine on Stavros!
jokes.setup: Stavros told someone is dining and dashing
jokes.punchline: His mangled English version of the phrase
jokes.explanation: Standard ethnic character wordplay. Functional but relies on familiar immigrant English patterns.

jokes.text: Someone's hitting my butt with something! Is frying pan!
jokes.setup: Homer stuck in bathroom being attacked
jokes.explanation: Standard slapstick with ethnic character voice. Physical comedy is solid but not innovative.

jokes.text: Leave your ass, Homer. Leave your ass.
jokes.explanation: Decent wordplay on 'assets' vs anatomy. The repetition adds to the ethnic character voice.

jokes.text: which I am already wearing, for only 3.95?
jokes.setup: Comic Book Guy negotiating for Power Ranger underwear
jokes.punchline: Admitting he's already put them on
jokes.explanation: Good visual gag implied by dialogue. Comic Book Guy's shameless admission fits his character.

jokes.text: And that came from Mr. Flanders buying back his own stuff.
jokes.punchline: Revealing Flanders bought his own possessions
jokes.explanation: Good Flanders character moment - his politeness preventing him from refusing to buy his own things.

jokes.text: I'm breaking in a new pair of flip flops, and my thong notch is on fire!
jokes.setup: Lenny explaining why he needs pain medication
jokes.explanation: Good mundane pain leading to drug purchase. The specific 'thong notch' terminology works.

jokes.text: Drugs in a bottle. I feel like Elvis Presley.
jokes.punchline: Comparing himself to Elvis's drug problems
jokes.explanation: Dark but good reference connecting prescription abuse to celebrity overdose. Good economy.

jokes.text: It's for... a friend. Who's trapped in the body of another friend.
jokes.setup: Moe buying estrogen and being questioned
jokes.explanation: Good escalation of obvious lie. Moe's tendency to dig deeper holes is well-established.

jokes.text: Can't talk now, Homer. I'm too satisfied in every way.
jokes.setup: Homer calls Lenny in Vegas expecting boredom
jokes.punchline: Lenny's complete satisfaction with Vegas experience
jokes.explanation: Good payoff to Homer's assumption Vegas would be boring. Lenny's complete satisfaction is perfect.

jokes.text: Shower you with love, because this experience has taught me just how precious you are. I'll kill you! I'll kill your whole family!
jokes.setup: Homer promises to be loving if Bart saves him
jokes.explanation: Great character work - Homer's inability to maintain fake sentiment. The contrast structure works well.

jokes.text: That's the beauty of a shallow grave. You sweet little angel.
jokes.setup: Homer threatening to kill and bury Bart repeatedly
jokes.punchline: Explaining shallow grave logistics then switching to endearment
jokes.explanation: Brilliant dark humor with perfect Homer logic about murder efficiency. The tonal whiplash is expertly done.

jokes.text: It's a reference to Ma Kettle. A popular movie character from the '40s. If you have to explain it, it's not good, chief.
jokes.setup: Wiggum makes obscure reference, then explains it
jokes.punchline: Lou pointing out the comedy rule about explanations
jokes.explanation: Excellent meta-humor about comedy itself. The show commenting on its own joke while making a better one.

jokes.text: Being a father is just a job. Long hours, no pay, and at the end, all you get is someone yelling, 'you screwed me up'!
jokes.punchline: Cynical but accurate description of parenthood
jokes.explanation: Good observational humor with real insight. Homer's rare moment of clarity about parenting reality.

jokes.text: Being a father is just a job. Long hours, no pay, and at the end, all you get is someone yelling, 'you screwed me up'!
jokes.explanation: Good observational humor - parenting as thankless job description

jokes.text: This is a boy scout uniform that I dolled up.
jokes.explanation: Good revelation of camp's fraudulent nature. The casual admission works for the character.

jokes.text: Hey, Homer, you wanna eat my shorts?
jokes.explanation: Nice emotional use of established catchphrase. Shows affection through familiar antagonism.

jokes.text: I bet this looks cool from overhead, though.
jokes.setup: Homer crashes and gets back in car
jokes.punchline: Commenting on the visual from animation perspective
jokes.explanation: Good meta-joke about animation's visual possibilities. Homer accidentally being director-aware.

jokes.text: I'm gonna order a steak dinner and eat it on the toilet!
jokes.explanation: Perfect Homer grossness - combines gluttony with poor hygiene as ideal

jokes.text: I'm gonna order a steak dinner and eat it on the toilet!
jokes.explanation: Perfect Homer excess - combining fine dining with complete lack of class. Very quotable.

jokes.text: Also, I don't know where Bart is. If you see him, say 'hey.'
jokes.setup: Homer calling from jail about serious situation
jokes.punchline: Casual afterthought about losing his son
jokes.explanation: Brilliant Homer priorities - jail is serious, losing child is casual greeting request. Perfect character logic.

jokes.text: Well, Maggie, I always knew it would some day boil down to just you and me. I'll look for work in the morning.
jokes.setup: Homer and Bart are in jail
jokes.explanation: Great Marge moment - her practical response to family disaster. The casual job search planning is perfect.

tmdb_overview: After Bart steals a key from Principal Skinner's office and gets himself into hot water, Marge and Homer send him to a behavior modification camp.
